coredata.dc:title: Enhance the electrical conductivity and charge storage of nematic phase by doping 0D photoluminescent graphene was prepared with small organic molecule as a new array quantum dot liquid crystal displays
coredata.dc:creator: Dalir, Nima
Javadian, Soheila
Kakemam, Jamal
Morteza Sadrpoor, S.
coredata.dc:description: The E7 Nematic liquid crystal (NLC) doped with Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been obtanied by bottom-up approaches to improve thermodynamics and electrochemical properties. The NI phase transition temperature and enthalpy were increased in comparison with the pure E7. The anchoring effect plays a key role in NI phase transition Also, the Ea values was decreased in doped GQDs system comapre to pure E7. The electrical conductivity of the doped GQD system was significantly more than pure E7. GQDs doping does significantly affect the permittivity of the nematic host, it noticeably increases its charge capacitance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nematic liquid crystal
Graphene quantum dots
Thermodynamics and electrochemical

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermodynamic and electrochemical behaviour of E7 LCs doped with GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of CA and GQDs, (b) X-ray diffraction pattern of GQDs, (c) DLS size distribution of GQDs, (d) Raman spectra of GQDs and pristine CA (e) UV–Vis absorption of CA and the GQDs and (f) exaction and emission spectra of GQDs.

(a) Heating DSC thermograms with 5 K min−1 scan rate, (b) Heating comparative semi-ln plot of scan ramp rate (K·min−1) versus 1/T for all samples, (c) Cole-Cole plots of the pure and the GQDs doped samples and (d) Overall resistance value for the E7 and GQDs-NLC doped with different ratio of GQDs.
(a) & (b) Heating DSC thermograms with 5 K min−1 scan rate (c) & (d) Cole-Cole plots of the pure E 7, the CA doped and traces solvent samples.
POM image of pure E7 LC's and all doped GQDs into E7 LCs.
Preparation of GQDs by using CA at 200 °C.
Construction of LC's cells for dielectric measurements.
Summary the thermodynamics parameter of pure E7 LCs and GQDs doped into NLC's.
Impedance parameter based on EEC model by fitting experimental data in the 100 mHz to 100 KHz frequency rang.

head.ce:title: Enhance the electrical conductivity and charge storage of nematic phase by doping 0D photoluminescent graphene was prepared with small organic molecule as a new array quantum dot liquid crystal displays

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The E7 Nematic liquid crystal (NLC) doped with Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been obtanied by bottom-up approaches to improve thermodynamics and electrochemical properties. The NI phase transition temperature and enthalpy were increased in comparison with the pure E7. The anchoring effect plays a key role in NI phase transition Also, the Ea values was decreased in doped GQDs system comapre to pure E7. The electrical conductivity of the doped GQD system was significantly more than pure E7. GQDs doping does significantly affect the permittivity of the nematic host, it noticeably increases its charge capacitance.

ce:section.ce:para: A combination of inorganic functional nanoparticles with organic soft materials such as block copolymers [1,2] and liquid crystals (LCs) [3,4] is a promising approach to design materials with novel electronic, optical, and thermal properties. The function of Nematic liquid crystal (NLC) devices mainly depends on some physical parameters such as threshold voltage, voltage holding ratio (VHR), birefringence, dielectric anisotropy (Δε), and NI phase transition temperature. The physiochemical properties of LCs can be change by dopant materials [5].

In the past decade, for improvement of LCs properties were used nanoparticles (NPs) [6] and nanomaterial [7] doping as a simple way. The LCs doped with Au, Ag, and TiO2 NPs were investigated for possible application in liquid crystal displays (LCDs) resulted in notable improvement of their properties [8]. The response time of LCs were improved with addition of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [9]. In the nanomaterials, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have made very interested. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of NLC were investigated by doped functionalized Cadmium Selenium (CdSe) and Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) quantum dots (QDs) [10]. The inclusion of semiconductor QDs into NLC affects the orientation of the LC molecules from planar to homeotropic depending on the size, nature, and concentration of QDs [11].
The GQDs are new dopants material into NLC and with a high specific surface area, large diameter, and fine surface grafting through π-π conjugated and other physicochemical properties [12].

By using two top-down and bottom-up methods the GQDs was synthesized. In the top-down and bottom-up methods were used cutting large Graphene and organic molecules with carbon source for perpetration of GQDs respectively [13]. The main unique properties of GQDs include high conductivity, large specific surface area, and suitable chemical stability. To the best of our knowledge, a few studies was used a GQDs for dopant material into NLC. Recently, were reported decreeing response time and threshold voltage of NLCs by doping of ZnO NPs with GQDs shell and Graphene oxide (GO) [14,15]. The PL intensity, electro-optic response, nonlinear dual photoluminescence and charge transport of ferroelectric liquid crystal (FLC) were improved significantly when doped with GQDs, GO and Graphene [16–21]. However, it has not been addressed in any previous report the effect of GQDs in the phase behavior and electrical properties of NLC's. Here, we report the effect of small flake GQDs on the phase transition behaviors, thermodynamic properties, and electrical conductivity of E7 NLC.

In this paper was used direct pyrolyzing of citric acid (CA) for preparation of GQDs as shown in Scheme 1 . The first, 2 g of CA was put into a 5 mL beaker then heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle and was liquated about 5 min later. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange in 30 min, implying the formation of GQDs. For preparing GQDs, the obtained orange liquid was added dropwise into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. After neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH, the solution of GQDs was obtained [13]. The percentage of unchanged material, Citric acid, and purity of GQDs were determined by using High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and the purity of GQDs was calculated 90.03% (as shown in Fig. 2S, 3S and 4S in ESI). Then, different ratios of GQDs were added into E7 NLC (0.5%, 1% and 1.5% w/w). All methods details have been reports in the ESI.

The obtained GQDs and CA have been characterized by using FT-IR spectroscopy as shown in (Fig. 1a). It was found that CA showed the broadening of OH stretching (R-C(O)-OH) at around 3290 cm−1, CO stretching (C(O)-OH) at 1721 cm−1, C-OH stretching at 1105 cm−1, and CH2 rocking at 778 cm−1. The result is attributed to the vibrational modes of citric acid molecule. The GQDs show an absorption peaks for carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at 3420 cm−1. The origin of this peaks were related to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CA. The band at 2950 and 1380 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of CH, which is present on the surface of GQDs, suggesting that the GQDs contain some incompletely carbonized CA. The absorption observed at 1650 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of the CC bond with Graphene structure. While, absorption peak at 830 cm−1 is attributed to the aromatic CH2 rocking [22]. It should be noted that no characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compound (stretching vibration of CH in aromatic rings around 3000–3100 cm−1, skeletal vibration of aromatic rings around 1450–1650 cm−1) are included in these FT-IR spectra, suggesting that no any separate aromatic compound is contained in the obtained CQDs.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of GQDs shows a wide (002) peak around 0.28 nm (Fig. 1b), can be suggested would produce graphite structures by carbonizing of CA. The layer of GQDs was determined by X-ray method is 1.80 based on Debye Scherer Eq are shown in Table 1S in ESI [23]. When GQDs was papered by CA the position of shift to higher value due to the disordered structure of GQDs, as a result of presence of great deal of oxygenated functional groups in their structure, which can cause a lack of considerable order in the GQDs [24]. Also, the average particles size for GQDs is 0.70 nm according to the dynamic light scattering (DLS) result (Fig. 1c) it was agreement the size of GQDs has been reported 1 nm at literature [25]. The D and G bands of GQDs were observed at 1346 and 1576 cm−1, respectively based on Raman analyses. The G band at 1576 cm−1 is attributed to the movement in opposed direction of two carbon atoms in the graphitic layer. The peak related to D band at 1346 cm−1 is associated to the amorphous/disordered carbon. The D and G bands (ID/IG) ratio is attributed to the ratio of amorphous carbon (sp3) to graphitic carbon (sp2). The value of (ID/IG) and I2D/IG were calculated 1.25 and 0.97 for GQDs respectively based on maximum intensity. Also, based on the ratio of I2D to IG in the Raman spectrum, the GQD was estimated to be 2-layer as shown Table 1S in ESI. Also, Raman spectra of GQDs was compared to pristine CA as shown in Fig. 1d [26]. The small peaks in the Raman spectra is corresponding to the some incompletely carbonized CA in the GQDs structure. The emission (blue light) and excitation of GQDs were occurred at 460 nm 360 nm UV beam respectively, as shown in Fig. 1e, f. The GQDs contain a lot of sp2 structures and functional groups. Consequently, the GQDs should share the same luminescence origin of other luminescent GQDs. However, except for giving blue light emission under the excitation by 360 nm UV light, there is some significant differences in optical properties between the GQDs [27]. The size and shape, in turn, determine the properties and applications of Graphene. For example, GQDs has photoluminescence properties [27], whereas Graphene sheets do not, due to their metallic character [28]. Due to the convergence valence and conduction band in Dirac point a pristine Graphene sheet have a zero-band-gap [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic and electrochemical behaviour of E7 LCs doped with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The phase transition temperatures of E7 NLC doped with GQDs are examined in heating and cooling cycles through DSC at the scan rate of 5 K·min−1 as shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. 8S. The NI phase transition was occurs at TN-I 333.02 K in pristine E7 NLC, while this transition are increased up to 1% of GQDs ratio. However, higher doping concentration of GQDs leads to the further decreased in transition temperature. The effect of pristine CA and traces solvent on the phase transitions of E7 LC's as shown in Fig. 3a & b. In the present of pristine CA the NI phase transition temperature was decreased while it was increased in the present of GQDs. Also, the trace solvent's effect is negligible on the E7 LC's properties. The strong π–π interaction of the aromatic cores comprised of phenyl rings of the LC molecules with the honey-comb pattern of the GQDs induces alignment of along the nematic director. The shift in NI transition temperatures is supposed to be induced by the π–π interaction between the E7 molecules and GQDs particles. The GQDs may promote nematic order in E7 NLC with extrapolation lengths of the local anchoring field. We suggested for E7 + GQDs the LC orientations at the GQDs surface can be quasi-random due to a degeneracy of possible orientations [30–32]. Whereas in the LC bulk these orientations would be uniform parallel to the GQDs surface, but at the surface there would be some degree of quenched random-field disordering [30,33]. This phenomena can be increased the NI transition temperatures in the E7 + GQDs compare to pure system. According to Table 1 , the enthalpy of NI phase transition of E7 + GQDs is initially increased by increasing GQDs ratio. Also, the same trend was observed for entropy. Increasing doped GQDs into the E7 enhances the intermolecular interactions compared to pure E7 since GQDs increases the contact area of interaction. Also, the ordering of NLCs is strictly increased around of GQDs by π-π interaction. The activation energy was calculated in different ramp rates (Fig. 7S). By variation of phase transition vs. scan rate have been calculated activation energy as follows:
(1)
β =
β 0
exp
−
Δ E
RT
where β is the effective rate (in K·min−1) and β0 is a constant (in K·min−1), ∆E is the activation energy (J.mol−1), R is universal gas constant (J.mol−1.K−1) and T is absolute temperature (K). The mentioned equation can be rewritten as:
(2)
Lnβ = Ln
β 0
−
Δ E
RT

The activation energy and lnβ0 was obtained from the slope and intercept of the plot lnβ vs 1/T. The activation energy is obtained from the slope of mentioned diagrams as shown in Fig. 2b. The value of activation energy can help us to speculate about the energy attributable to a molecular rotational mode. In other words, the configurations in the two phases were separated by activation energy. Accordingly, the transition is expected to become sharper as the heating rate is reduced and the molecules have time to respond to the gradual changes in temperature [34]. According to Table 1, the activation energy of E7 + GQDs is less as compared to E7 which is mainly due to the spinning of aromatic biphenyl rings around a specific axe. This spinning is strictly dependent on the hydrocarbon chain freedom rotation degree. Specially, the flip-flop motions of LC molecules result in a decrease of activation energy. In the LC's-GQDs composite the π-π stacking between the LC's-GQDs causes the freedom rotation of hydrocarbon chain is more easily than pure LC's. In other words, the degree of quenched random-field disordering was decreased. As a resulted by decreasing activation energy, the ordering of LC's molecules are increase [35].

The electrochemical measurements were carried out in sandwich cells with transparent electrodes, and electric field applied out-of-plane (OP) cell, i.e., perpendicularly to the cells (Scheme 2 more description was reported in ESI). The Cole-Cole plots of doped and pure LC's are shown in Fig. 2c have been indicated by non-Debye type relaxation. In the Cole–Cole plots in which the imaginary (Zim) part is plotted against the real part (Zr) of the impedance. Z r and Z im were computed at different frequencies and the equivalent electrical circuit (EEC) includes a semicircle with diameter R LC. The both real and imaginary parts of impedance have been shifted to lower values by in increasing the GQDs ratio.

The effect of pristine CA and traces solvent on Cole-Cole plots of E7 LC's are shown in Fig. 3c & d. The pristine CA was increased the real and imaginary part of impedance compare to both pure E7 and GQDs, while the effect of traces solvent is negligible. The EEC model comprise a resistor RCR for the resistivity of cell electrodes and connectors, in series with a parallel set of capacitor CLC and resistor RLC, resembling the real and imaginary behaviors of the LCs. In the low frequency range three component circuit including paralleled bulk capacitance CLC and resistivity RLC with capacitance CDL have been modelled in series [20,36]. The capacitance element CDL was added in series to CLC and RLC by accumulation of space-charge near the electrodes. The mentioned EEC has been extended by double layer of charged alignment agent to finite the dielectric LC and a resistor RLC and capacitance CLC in series.

For investigation of impedance response in the low frequency range base on above mentioned EEC model should be considered the ion drift and accumulation of space charge near the electrodes. The CDL and Warburg coefficient (W) denote the capacitance of the space charge and drift of ions within confined geometry modelled by a generalized finite space, respectively.

The experimental impedance data were fitted based on EEC model with high accuracy are summarized in Table 2 . The GQDs ratios does not significantly affect the resistance RCR = 2.59 ± 0.62 kΩ. Also, under different ratio of GQDs into E7 LCs the value of CLC is constant 36.14 ± 0.7 pF and small deviation is expected from slightly different cell gaps. A strong effect of GQDs doping is found for the RLC, as it decreased from 1.76 MΩ for pristine E7 to 0.98 MΩ for 1.5% GQDs. Since the RLC value is decreased by doping of GQDS, it can be concluded that GQDs doped E7 composites can be applied in circuits working at high sensitive system. In other words, by increasing conductivity as a function of GQDs ratio in the composite system the LC's molecule can be shows higher response to electrical field compare to pure E7 LC's. In other word the LC's molecules is sensitive (respect to electrical field) in the present of GQDs. Also based on Table 2 in doped system the value of capacitance was increased. The value of CDL have been increased with GQDs ratio rise up to 1% (w/w) and based on EEC model the CDL was determined about 2.63 ± 1.9 nF. When the LC's molecules presence in the interface interact with GQDs under electric field, then double-layer was formed in the near of electrode and in LC's medium. As a resulted by increasing GQDs ratio was increased the value of double-layer capacitance. Are shown in Fig. 2d the overall impedance behaviour of pristine and doped E7 LCs.

The overall impedance value of pristine E7 LCs in the 100 mHz to 4.5 Hz frequency range was decreased. This value at the 4.5 Hz to 30 Hz regions significantly was reached a plateau of a quasi-constant. Additionally, can be seen a linear decrease of overall impedance value in the 30 Hz to 2.2 kHz frequency ranges. Finally in the above 2.2 kHz the overall impedance value is constant. The overall impedance value of E7 + GQDs have been decreased by increasing frequency below of 4.5 Hz. Also, this value shows flattens to a quasi-constant plateau and were shifted to lower frequencies compare to pristine E7 LCs in the 4.5–30 Hz frequency region.

Additionally, at frequency range of about 30 Hz–2.2 kHz, the overall impedance was increased by increasing GQDs ratio compare to pristine E7 NLCs. This behaviour can be assumed as a characteristic phenomenon for capacitors in which the charge storage is intensified consequently. Finally, the behaviour of overall impedance of E7 + GQDs in the above 2.5 kHz was corresponds to that of pristine E7 NLCs.

Based on the polarized optical microscopy (POM) results (Fig. 4 ), the aggregation was formed between GQDs molecules when the GQDs ratio was increased up to 1%. As a result, the saturation behaviour in the electrical characteristics for the 1% and 1.5% of GQDs was observed due to aggregation formation.

In summary, we have demonstrated that GQDs doped NLCs show a higher TN-I phase transition compared to pure E7 NLCs. Thermodynamic parameters such as ΔH and ΔS were dramatically increased in the doped system. The impedance spectroscopy technique was applied to study the electrical specificities of mentioned compounds indicating that the doped GQDs have increased. Also, the conductivity significantly which is mainly attributed to implementation of GQDs particles in LCs which increase the ionic conduction in addition to the impurity of ions compared to pure E7 NLCs. In the E7 + GQDs doped system, the NI phase transition was increased up to 1% ratio of GQDs. In other words, the π-π stacking between LC's-GQDs ordering of LC's molecule give rise to an increase of the NI phase transition temperature. Moreover, this interaction enhances the double-layer capacitance too. According to the results, GQDs doped NLCs show better performance resulting in noticeable improvement in main electrical properties of NLCs. We believe that the present work provides valuable insight into the design of organic insulating materials with high electrical conductivity and thermal properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to Prof. Y. Yamini and Miss. M. Karami at Tarbiat Modares University for his assistance to this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: See the Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) for the experimental section and characterization of GQDs. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2018.11.160.
